replacement_html:
4.2.2 What the globe predicts

The dome publishes a falsification rule that says standard physics predicts zero — “measured anomaly within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction)” falsifies ECM. That premise is wrong: standard physics (the 90-year-old Chapman–Sq mechanism) predicts dips of 5–25 nT and observatories have measured them across dozens of eclipses. The dome’s own BOU 2017 baseline (−10.9 nT) is one of those Chapman measurements, so by its own published criterion the dome has already falsified the “globe prediction” it claims to compete with.

The dome’s eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. The dome’s live predictive-power dashboard publishes the eclipse falsification criterion verbatim: “Eclipse 2026 — Fails if measured anomaly is within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction).” The eclipse countdown banner on the same page formalizes the comparison: “ECM predicts -[value] nT, globe predicts 0 nT. Requires Kp < 2.” (live URL, captured 2026-04-25). Both statements assert that conventional physics expects no eclipse-induced magnetic perturbation. Neither is supported by the literature.

This framing is what makes the dome’s prediction look discriminating. If the globe is held to predict zero, then any non-zero quiet-day measurement at the prediction stations becomes “confirmation of ECM, falsification of globe.” If the actual globe prediction is 5–25 nT — overlapping the dome’s −5 to −26 nT registered range almost entirely (see 4.2.3) — the comparison collapses: both models predict the same sign and similar magnitude, and the eclipse can no longer distinguish between them.

What standard physics actually predicts. Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first identified by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the Moon’s shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-layer conductivity (at 90–150 km altitude) and disrupting the solar quiet (Sq) current system. This produces measurable ground-level magnetic perturbations that track the eclipse shadow geometry. Modern implementations of the Chapman mechanism — particularly the Ashour-Chapman models refined by Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) — match observed eclipse magnetic signals “strikingly accurately” at INTERMAGNET stations, predicting magnitudes of 5–25 nT depending on eclipse coverage, station latitude, local ionospheric conditions, and Sq current geometry. The literature is extensive: 207 observations across 39 eclipses (1991–2016) and a multi-station INTERMAGNET study of 4 total eclipses confirm the mechanism quantitatively. The dome’s site separately characterizes the globe prediction as having “no physical mechanism” for eclipse magnetic effects; in fact the Chapman Sq-current mechanism has been refined for over 90 years and quantitatively predicts both the magnitude and the shadow-tracking geometry of these signals.

By the dome’s own published criterion, it has already falsified itself. The dome’s BOU 2017 baseline of −10.9 nT (used as the input to its own eclipse formula — see 4.2.5) is itself a Chapman-mechanism measurement on a spherical Earth at a quiet-day INTERMAGNET station. It sits well outside the dome’s stated “globe prediction” window of ±3 nT around zero. The dome can either accept its own falsification criterion — in which case the “globe predicts 0” framing is wrong and the eclipse comparison was never discriminating — or revise the criterion, in which case the falsification rule it publishes to the public is not the rule it actually applies. Either resolution removes the eclipse from the model-discriminating column.

tldr_change.old_tldr: Standard ionospheric physics (the Chapman-Sq mechanism, known since 1933) already predicts magnetic dips of 5-25 nT during solar eclipses from reduced ionospheric conductivity. This has been measured and confirmed across dozens of eclipses over 90 years.
tldr_change.new_tldr_lead: The dome publishes a falsification rule that says standard physics predicts zero — “measured anomaly within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction)” falsifies ECM. That premise is wrong: standard physics (the 90-year-old Chapman–Sq mechanism) predicts dips of 5–25 nT and observatories have measured them across dozens of eclipses. The dome’s own BOU 2017 baseline (−10.9 nT) is one of those Chapman measurements, so by its own published criterion the dome has already falsified the “globe prediction” it claims to compete with.
tldr_change.rationale: Operator directive: lead with misstatement before the supporting Chapman content. Punchline first — the dome has self-falsified by its own published rule.

holes_addressed: Section 4.2.2 buried the lead — the load-bearing error (dome misstates globe as predicting 0 nT) was not in the section that establishes "what the globe predicts."
Hedging analysis in 4.2.3 reads as a separate critique rather than a downstream consequence of the misstatement; cross-reference now ties them together.
Reader had no verbatim dome quote to anchor the misstatement claim — both citable quotes from live.html are now in-page.
BOU 2017 self-falsification observation (already implicit in 4.2.3 + 4.2.5) is now made explicit at the section that owns the globe-prediction framing.

Verbatim dome quote 2 (eclipse countdown banner JS, live.html, 2026-04-25 capture): "ECM predicts -[value] nT, globe predicts 0 nT. Requires Kp < 2."

Live URL anchor: https://john09289.github.io/predictions/live.html (cross-checked against poller-archived raw-text/08-live-power.txt lines 104 and 190; both quotes byte-identical to current live.html as of 2026-04-25T~21:00Z).

anticipated_objections.objection: Maybe the dome means "the globe predicts no SPECIFICALLY ECM-style aetheric magnetic anomaly" rather than "the globe predicts zero magnetic perturbation during eclipse."
anticipated_objections.response: The published falsification criterion is unconditional — "within ±3 nT of 0" — with no qualifier about mechanism. A reading where the dome means something other than what it published would require the dome to actually publish that other meaning. Until it does, the literal text governs. If the dome amends the criterion in response, that itself becomes evidence (timestamped via OTS on status_history.json — see Timestamp Error tab) that the original was unsupported.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 5-25 nT Chapman range is itself a synthesis — different sources give different bands.
anticipated_objections.response: Range is conservative and well-cited (Meza et al. 2021; 207-observation review; multi-station INTERMAGNET study). Even if the lower bound were tightened to a few nT or the upper bound widened to ~50 nT for high-Sq stations, the ±3 nT around zero claim remains incompatible with the literature. The mechanism does not predict zero; it predicts a non-zero, sign-stable, geometry-tracking signal.

anticipated_objections.objection: BOU 2017 was a disturbed-day measurement, so it does not falsify a quiet-day prediction.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome itself uses BOU 2017 as one of its two registered baselines (alongside W004 empirical, see raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 23). If BOU 2017 is too disturbed to count as a Chapman measurement, it is also too disturbed to anchor the dome formula. The dome cannot have it both ways — either −10.9 nT is a meaningful eclipse signal (in which case it falsifies "globe predicts 0") or it is not (in which case the dome formula has no input baseline). Caveat noted in WIN-010 source description ("disturbed-day baseline") supports the former reading.

anticipated_objections.objection: The misstatement is only on live.html (Domain 11 panel + countdown banner), not on the dome's main predictions page or the eclipse-specific writeup.
anticipated_objections.response: Curmudgeon should verify scope. If the misstatement is limited to live.html, the framing should be "the dome's live falsification dashboard misstates standard physics" rather than "the dome's eclipse argument depends on..." Current draft uses the strong form because the live page is the dome's public-facing scoreboard and the falsification list is what the dome offers to skeptics as evidence of testability. Soften if curmudgeon finds the framing genuinely weakens elsewhere.

kernel_of_truth: Publishing an explicit numerical falsification criterion is methodologically sound — the dome is doing what most pseudoscience refuses to do. The Domain 11 falsification rule ("Fails if measured anomaly is within ±3 nT of 0") is exactly the kind of quantitative pre-commitment that distinguishes testable claims from un-testable ones. The dome should be credited for publishing such criteria across all 14+ domains.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: A falsification criterion that compares your prediction against a misstated alternative is not a real test — it is a strawman dressed in test-statistic clothing. The dome correctly publishes a number; the number just compares ECM to a fictional version of standard physics. The criterion needs to compare ECM against the actual Chapman prediction (5-25 nT range, sign-stable, shadow-tracking) to be discriminating. Until then, the visible methodology is sound but the pre-registered comparison is not.

cross_references.notes: Hedging analysis in 4.2.3 only reads as hedging if the alternative is misstated as zero — without the misstatement, the dome's -5 to -26 nT range overlaps the 5-25 nT Chapman range and the dual-baseline structure becomes a normal prediction with overlapping confidence intervals, not a hedge. Cross-reference inserted as a single sentence at the top of p4-423 ps-detail (see secondary_patch).

BOU 2017 -10.9 nT figure already documented in 4.2.5 as a Chapman-mechanism measurement repurposed as dome baseline. New 4.2.2 prose adds a forward link "see 4.2.5" so reader can follow the structural argument from misstatement (4.2.2) → hedging (4.2.3) → no-dome-geometry-in-formula (4.2.5).

When EXP-254 (eclipse geometric trilemma) lands, 4.2.7 will reference 4.2.2's misstatement framing per HNOTE-OPERATOR-NEW-SECTION-4-2-7-ECLIPSE-GEOMETRY-001-AMENDMENT-001. No coordination required at draft time; 4.2.7 forward-references this section.

Apply DIRECTIVE-20260422-003 design rubric (TLDR readability, prose cadence, ps-detail formatting, no double-em-dash, term-class consistency).

Stress-test the kernel-of-truth section: is publishing a falsification criterion really methodologically sound, or is the criterion construction (compare to fictional alternative) so flawed that the publishing of a number is itself misleading? Operator-friendly counterargument is the steel-man; curmudgeon should test whether the kernel survives that test.
